bare: - Usher, L.V. et al. (2022). "Conversational Adaptation in Children and Teens with Autism: Differences in Talkativeness across Contexts." *Autism Research*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aur.2757)
claim_context: NT participants adapted talkativeness based on partner cues (more when interested, less when bored); autistic participants did not differentially adapt — cited for failure to adapt to listener interest.
source_says: The actual paper with this title and findings is Cola et al. (2022), Autism Research, DOI 10.1002/aur.2693, PMC9167260. It describes exactly the bored-vs-interested talkativeness finding.
claim_support: Claim supported by the real paper, but this citation is MISATTRIBUTED: first author is Cola, not Usher; DOI aur.2757 does not exist. Should read Cola, M. et al. (2022), aur.2693.
notes: AUTHOR/DOI MISATTRIBUTION. Cola et al. (2022) is already correctly cited in category-07-register.md. Replace 'Usher' attribution in category-13-topic-research-theory.md.
impact_if_removed: Low — correctly-attributed Cola 2022 already used elsewhere; replace, don't remove.

impact_if_removed: Low-moderate — book version (2012) covers similar ground.

bare: - Wehrle et al. (2023): "Receptive prosody exceeds expressive prosody in ASD"
claim_context: Summary claim based on Wehrle 2023 PEPS-C findings.
source_says: Partial match — the PEPS-C paper reports receptive prosody deficits in contrastive stress AND expressive deficits (imitation, lexical/contrastive stress). Receptive-exceeds-expressive is an oversimplification but compatible with parts of the data.
claim_support: Partial support; phrasing is a slight oversimplification of Wehrle's nuanced finding.
notes: Framing slightly stronger than paper supports — paper found both receptive and expressive differences, not a clean dissociation.

notes: This confirms Wilson & Bishop 2021 exists and supports adult implied-meaning processing claims.

bare: - Wilson, A.C. et al. (2021). "Second guessing yourself all the time about what they really mean..." *Autism Research*.
claim_context: Cognitive differences between autistic and non-autistic adults in understanding implied meaning.
source_says: Verified: Wilson & Bishop (2021) Autism Research 14(1):93-101, DOI 10.1002/aur.2345, PMID 32686325. Autistic participants 5x more likely to select 'do not know' on implicature tasks.
claim_support: Directly supports adult implied-meaning processing claim.
notes: Per INSTRUCTIONS: 'Wilson & Bishop 2021/2022 verification result' — CONFIRMED this paper exists and supports claim.

bare: **Wilson & Bishop (2022)** — N = 102 autistic adults, 109 controls
claim_context: Inline reference to Wilson & Bishop 2022 Stage 2 Registered Report sample size.
source_says: Verified: Wilson & Bishop (2022) PeerJ 10:e13110, DOI 10.7717/peerj.13110, PMC8919847. N=102 autistic, 109 non-autistic.
notes: Slight discrepancy in numbers: one search result said 'autistic adults (N = 66) were 6.19 times more likely... compared to non-autistic people (N = 118)' — but the 2022 paper reports 102/109. The 66/118 numbers may be from the 2021 paper. Need to verify which paper's N=102/109 refers to.

bare: - Wilson, A.C. & Bishop, D.V.M. (2022). "'Second guessing yourself...'" *PeerJ*, 10, e13110.
claim_context: PeerJ Stage 2 Registered Report on preference for certainty in conversation among autistic adults.
source_says: Verified: Wilson & Bishop (2022) PeerJ 10:e13110, PMC8919847. IMPORTANT: the title 'Second guessing yourself all the time...' is actually the 2021 Autism Research paper (aur.2345). The 2022 PeerJ e13110 paper has a DIFFERENT title: 'Stage 2 registered report: investigating a preference for certainty in conversation among autistic adults'.
claim_support: The 2022 PeerJ paper exists and is by Wilson & Bishop. But the HUB has MERGED the 2021 Autism Research paper's title with the 2022 PeerJ DOI — that's a minor misattribution of title across years.
notes: TITLE/YEAR MIX-UP. Hub conflates two related Wilson & Bishop papers: (1) Autism Research 2021 aur.2345 'Second guessing yourself...' and (2) PeerJ 2022 e13110 'Stage 2 registered report: ... preference for certainty'. Both exist; both support implied-meaning/certainty claims. Separate them properly.

bare: - Zalta et al. (2021). — ScienceDirect [paywalled]
claim_context: Short-form Zalta 2021 citation with ScienceDirect URL.
source_says: URL S0272735821000416 is actually Wang, Chung, Wang, Yu & Kenardy (2021) 'Social support and PTSD: A meta-analysis of longitudinal studies', Clinical Psychology Review, PMID 33714168 — NOT Zalta. The real Zalta 2021 paper is in Psychological Bulletin.
claim_support: AUTHOR/URL MISATTRIBUTION. The Zalta paper exists but the linked URL points to Wang et al. 2021.
notes: URL MISATTRIBUTION. Zalta 2021 Psych Bulletin DOI is 10.1037/bul0000316 — this URL actually resolves to Wang et al. 2021 Clin Psych Rev. Both are real PTSD social-support meta-analyses but by different first authors.
impact_if_removed: Low — housekeeping; claim still supportable via correctly-cited Zalta or Wang papers.

key: zalta|2021|social support and ptsd a meta
bare: - Zalta, A. K., et al. (2021). Social support and PTSD: A meta-analysis of longitudinal studies.
claim_context: Cited as longitudinal meta-analysis with r≈-0.26 cross-sectional effect, reciprocal effects.
source_says: The URL S0272735821000416 and the description (longitudinal, reciprocal, r≈-0.26) match Wang et al. (2021) Clinical Psychology Review — NOT Zalta. The Zalta 2021 Psych Bulletin paper is a DIFFERENT meta-analysis (not longitudinal-specific).
claim_support: AUTHOR MISATTRIBUTION. The described findings match Wang et al. (2021) Clin Psych Rev, not Zalta et al.
notes: CORRECTION: first author should be Wang, Y. (Yabing Wang, with Chung, Wang, Yu & Kenardy), 2021 Clinical Psychology Review. PMID 33714168.
impact_if_removed: Low — the underlying claim (social support buffers PTSD longitudinally) is well-supported by the correctly-attributed Wang paper.

The 'Zeidan 2022' paper (aur.2696) is a DIFFERENT paper: 'Global prevalence of autism: A systematic review update', Autism Research.

key: zimmermann|2008|contrastive focus in c fery s
bare: - Zimmermann, M. (2008). Contrastive Focus. In C. Fery & S. Ishihara (Eds.), *Oxford Handbook of Information Structure*.
claim_context: Canonical theoretical reference on contrastive focus in information structure.
source_says: The Oxford Handbook of Information Structure (Fery & Ishihara eds.) was published 2016, not 2008. Zimmermann does have work on contrastive focus circa 2008 (e.g., Zimmermann 2008 Linguistic Review 'Contrastive Focus and Emphasis').
claim_support: Claim is likely supported but handbook year attribution is off — Oxford Handbook is 2016, not 2008. The 2008 Zimmermann contrastive focus paper is in The Linguistic Review.
notes: YEAR/VENUE ERROR. The 2008 Zimmermann contrastive focus piece is 'Contrastive Focus and Emphasis' in The Linguistic Review 25(3-4), NOT in Oxford Handbook of Info Structure (which is 2016).
